posts.text:
As Predicted, ICE Reports Another Detention Death While Congress Does Nothing: 2nd This Week, 13th This Year, 42nd Under Trump

austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

Salt Lake City Mayor Erin Mendenhall opposes ICE’s proposed detention facility, warning it could strain water systems, emergency services, and local infrastructure while costing over $1M annually in tax revenue. 

deseret.com/utah/2026/03/17/…

Economic and social class doesn't disappear when you cross a border, but it does change. A new special issue in the Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies puts class back at the center of how we understand migration. Here's why that matters.

The old binary of working-class vs. middle-class doesn't capture today's reality. Class is unstable, relational, and deeply shaped by where you are, who recognizes your credentials, and what assets your family holds.

If you want to understand migration in 2026, you have to take class seriously not as a fixed label, but as something made, unmade, and remade across borders. 

Full article here:
tandfonline.com/doi/full/10.…

Does ICE Have a 287(g) Agreement with an Elementary School? We Need Answers.

ICE just added the first K-12 school district to its 287(g) enforcement program—then removed it days later. What happened? Oklahomans, we need your help investigating. 

austinkocher.substack.com/p/…

Immigration detention is associated with PTSD, depression, and developmental regression in children. Medical consensus is clear: there are no safe conditions for detaining minors. 

thecut.com/article/immigrati…

Did we just see the first elementary school in the country sign onto a 287(g) agreement? Get the latest data and evidence in my conversation with Andrew Thrasher at 3 PM Eastern today. 

open.substack.com/live-strea…
Who do Americans want deported and does it matter if an immigrant is LGBTQ+? A new peer-reviewed study has surprising answers that complicate what politicians on both sides assume
Consensual same-sex activity remains illegal in over 60 countries worldwide. For LGBTQ+ immigrants, deportation isn't just removal, it can mean returning to places where their lives are at risk.

Public opinion on deportation is more complex than the debate suggests. Who gets seen as "deserving" to stay depends on party, identity, and framing not just facts. That has real consequences for real people.

Full study: journals.sagepub.com/doi/ful…

As Predicted, ICE Reports Another Detention Death While Congress Does Nothing: 2nd This Week, 13th This Year, 42nd Under Trump

austinkocher.substack.com/p/as-predict...

Public opinion on deportation is more complex than the debate suggests. Who gets seen as "deserving" to stay depends on party, identity, and framing not just facts. That has real consequences for real people.

Full study: journals.sagepub.com/doi/full/10....
Consensual same-sex activity remains illegal in over 60 countries worldwide. For LGBTQ+ immigrants, deportation isn't just removal, it can mean returning to places where their lives are at risk.

The researchers suggest partisans may assume immigrant sexuality signals ideological alignment, w/ Democrats perceiving gay immigrants as liberal allies, Republicans the opposite. Identity shapes who gets rewarded or punished.
Even with economic contributions shown, 50.8% of Republicans still supported deporting gay unauthorized immigrants compared to 44.4% for straight unauthorized immigrants. The gap persisted.
Among Republicans, the reverse was true. Economic contributions reduced deportation support for straight immigrants by 18.2 percentage points, but only 8.4 points for gay immigrants.
Among Democrats, economic contributions reduced deportation support for gay immigrants by 8.8 percentage points, compared to just 1.7 points for straight immigrants.
But that apparent consensus hides a sharp partisan divide. When you break the data down by party, Democrats and Republicans respond in nearly opposite ways to the same immigrant profiles.

With economic contributions highlighted, support for deporting gay immigrants fell from 37.5% to 29.1%. Support for deporting straight immigrants fell from 37.5% to 27.8%. The drop was real across the board.

What actually moved the needle? Economic contributions. When told an immigrant started a business creating 500+ jobs, support for deportation dropped significantly, for both gay and straight immigrants.

Without any other information, 37.5% of respondents supported deporting gay unauthorized immigrants, the exact same share supported deporting straight unauthorized immigrants. Sexual orientation alone changed nothing.

Researchers surveyed 2,100 U.S. respondents mirroring Census demographics. They tested whether an immigrant's sexual orientation changed how much people supported deporting them. The first finding may surprise you.

Deportation is not the same as opposing immigration. It means forcibly removing people already living here. Yet scholarship on what actually drives deportation attitudes has been almost nonexistent until now.

About 3% of all U.S. immigrants identify as LGBTQ+, and roughly 45% of those are estimated to be unauthorized. That means mass deportation could affect as many as 288,000 LGBTQ+ undocumented immigrants.
Who do Americans want deported and does it matter if an immigrant is LGBTQ+? A new peer-reviewed study has surprising answers that complicate what politicians on both sides assume

Salt Lake City Mayor Erin Mendenhall opposes ICE’s proposed detention facility, warning it could strain water systems, emergency services, and local infrastructure while costing over $1M annually in tax revenue. 

www.deseret.com/utah/2026/03...

If you want to understand migration in 2026, you have to take class seriously not as a fixed label, but as something made, unmade, and remade across borders.
